Repo: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1). Venv: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
/tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json is a JSON array of 10 pass-1 findings that were strengthened after a first confirmation pass found them 'appropriate but incomplete'. Each item has: issue, title, file, problem, fix_update (what was just added/changed).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for INDEX 3 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_reconfirm.json.
Read that item (issue, problem, fix_update). Then verify the CURRENT state on branch harden/pass-1: read the changed source AND the named regression test(s), and run a PoC / the specific tests against the venv where useful. Decide: appropriate = the fix soundly addresses the root cause (no hack/over-claim); complete = it fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would actually catch a regression (a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count). If the item documents a deliberately-deferred scope (e.g. #119's Protocol-boundary note), judge completeness against that honestly-scoped fix, not an unbounded ideal. Set either false with specifics if not met. Echo the issue number.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{'file': 'src/assay_engine/provenance.py',
 'fix_update': 'record() lock-guarded; NEW test '
               'test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency (8 threads x50, '
               'verify + contiguous seqs).',
 'issue': '114',
 'problem': 'record() computes `seq = len(self._entries)` and `prev_hash = '
            'self._entries[-1].entry_hash` (read), then does work (clock, '
            'digest, freeze), then `self._entries.append(entry)` (write), with '
            'no lock. If a single ProvenanceTrail were shared across threads, '
            'two concurrent record() calls could read the same seq/prev_hash '
            'and append two entries that both claim e.g. seq=N with the same '
            'prev_hash, breaking the chain so verify_records() (which asserts '
            'e.seq == i and e.prev_hash == prev) would fail — a co',
 'sev': 'LOW',
 'title': 'ProvenanceTrail.record performs a non-atomic check-then-act on '
          'shared list state (not thread-safe)'}

content.text: Issue #114. Let me read the source file and the regression test.

content.content:
harden/pass-1
"""Append-only provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3, W3C PROV-DM style).

Every action a study takes is recorded, in order, before the next executes: ingestion, the
blind baseline build, each discovered/locked hypothesis, every gate decision, each verdict,
the source scorecard, the final report. The trail is the spine of reproducibility and of the
independence claim — "no step can be retroactively removed or reordered" (GOVERNANCE §3).

The trail enforces that structurally, not by promise:

- **Append-only.** Entries are added through :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.record` only; there is no
  remove/edit/reorder API and the exposed view is an immutable tuple of frozen entries.
- **Chained.** Each entry carries ``prev_hash`` and an ``entry_hash`` over
  ``(prev_hash, seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp)`` using the engine's one type-faithful
  serializer (:mod:`assay_engine._canonical`), rooted at a fixed genesis.

Integrity — honest scope, two tiers:

- **Unkeyed (default).** The chain is plain SHA-256. It is *tamper-evident against naive
  tampering and accidental corruption*: editing one entry without recomputing downstream
  hashes, reordering, or deleting an entry is caught by :func:`verify_records`. It is **not**
  forgery-proof: a motivated party who controls the serialized bytes can edit a payload and
  recompute the whole genesis-rooted chain, and the result verifies. Unkeyed integrity buys
  "is this a valid chain?", not "is this *the* chain the engine produced."
- **Keyed (``secret=...``).** Pass an on-box secret (as pre-registration does, ADR-0009) and
  the chain is HMAC-SHA256. The head cannot be recomputed without the secret, so a downstream
  store cannot silently rewrite history and re-seal it; :func:`verify_records` must be given
  the same secret. This is local tamper-*resistance*; non-repudiable third-party attestation of
  the trail's time remains the same pluggable concern as pre-registration, out of scope here.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import datetime as _dt
import hashlib
import hmac
import threading
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Callable, Mapping

from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_json
from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping

_UTC = _dt.timezone.utc
_GENESIS = "assay-provenance:v1:genesis"

Clock = Callable[[], _dt.datetime]


class ProvenanceError(RuntimeError):
    """The provenance trail is inconsistent (a broken chain) or an entry cannot be recorded."""


@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class ProvenanceEntry:
    """One immutable, chained record of a single action."""

    seq: int
    kind: str
    summary: str
    payload: Mapping[str, Any]
    timestamp: str  # ISO-8601 UTC
    prev_hash: str
    entry_hash: str

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        object.__setattr__(self, "payload", freeze_mapping(self.payload))


def _digest(
    seq: int,
    kind: str,
    summary: str,
    payload: Mapping[str, Any],
    timestamp: str,
    prev_hash: str,
    secret: bytes | None,
) -> str:
    try:
        msg = canonical_json(
            ["assay-prov:v1", prev_hash, seq, kind, summary, dict(payload), timestamp]
        ).encode("utf-8")
    except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
        # RecursionError (deeply nested payload) is a RuntimeError, not Value/TypeError — catch it
        # too so the entrypoints surface a typed ProvenanceError, never a raw exception (#89).
        raise ProvenanceError(
            f"provenance payload for {kind!r} cannot be serialized reproducibly ({exc}); "
            "record only JSON-native / hashable values of bounded depth"
        ) from None
    if secret is None:
        return hashlib.sha256(msg).hexdigest()
    return hmac.new(secret, msg, "sha256").hexdigest()


def _utc_now() -> _dt.datetime:
    return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)


class ProvenanceTrail:
    """An append-only, chained audit trail for one study run.

    ``secret`` (>= 16 bytes) keys the chain with HMAC so a serialized trail cannot be forged
    without it (see module docstring). ``clock`` injects the timestamp source (deterministic
    tests); it must return a timezone-aware ``datetime``.
    """

    def __init__(self, *, secret: bytes | None = None, clock: Clock | None = None) -> None:
        if secret is not None and (not isinstance(secret, (bytes, bytearray)) or len(secret) < 16):
            raise ValueError("provenance secret must be >= 16 bytes of key material")
        self._entries: list[ProvenanceEntry] = []
        self._secret: bytes | None = bytes(secret) if secret is not None else None
        self._clock: Clock = clock or _utc_now
        # serialize the read-compute-append so concurrent record() calls cannot interleave and
        # corrupt the seq/prev_hash chain (#114)
        self._lock = threading.Lock()

    def record(self, kind: str, summary: str, **payload: Any) -> ProvenanceEntry:
        """Append one entry. Returns it. The only way to add to the trail. Thread-safe."""
        if not kind:
            raise ProvenanceError("provenance entry kind must be non-empty")
        with self._lock:
            return self._record_locked(kind, summary, payload)

    def _record_locked(
        self, kind: str, summary: str, payload: Mapping[str, Any]
    ) -> ProvenanceEntry:
        seq = len(self._entries)
        prev_hash = self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS
        try:
            instant = self._clock()
        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the clock is an injected dependency
            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance clock raised: {exc}") from None
        if not isinstance(instant, _dt.datetime):
            raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a datetime")
        try:
            # utcoffset()/astimezone()/isoformat() can raise on a hostile datetime subclass or an
            # out-of-range value — keep the typed-error contract (#96).
            if instant.tzinfo is None or instant.utcoffset() is None:
                raise ProvenanceError("provenance clock must return a timezone-aware datetime")
            timestamp = instant.astimezone(_UTC).isoformat()
        except ProvenanceError:
            raise
        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance timestamp could not be normalized: {exc}") from None
        entry_hash = _digest(seq, kind, summary, payload, timestamp, prev_hash, self._secret)
        try:
            entry = ProvenanceEntry(
                seq=seq,
                kind=kind,
                summary=summary,
                payload=payload,
                timestamp=timestamp,
                prev_hash=prev_hash,
                entry_hash=entry_hash,
            )
        except (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) as exc:
            raise ProvenanceError(
                f"provenance entry for {kind!r} could not be frozen ({exc})"
            ) from None
        self._entries.append(entry)
        return entry

    def as_recorder(self) -> Callable[[Any], None]:
        """A :data:`gates.ProvenanceRecorder` bound to this trail (records GateDecisions).

        Reads each attribute exactly once and defensively, so a booby-trapped decision object
        (a property that raises) surfaces a typed ``ProvenanceError`` rather than a raw error.
        """

        def _record(decision: Any) -> None:
            try:
                gate = getattr(decision, "gate", None)
                approved = bool(getattr(decision, "approved", False))
                reason = getattr(decision, "reason", "")
                evidence = dict(getattr(decision, "evidence", {}) or {})
            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — the decision is on the trust boundary
                raise ProvenanceError(
                    f"could not read gate decision for provenance: {exc}"
                ) from None
            self.record(
                "gate",
                f"gate {gate!r}: {'approved' if approved else 'blocked'}",
                gate=gate,
                approved=approved,
                reason=reason,
                evidence=evidence,
            )

        return _record

    @property
    def entries(self) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
        return tuple(self._entries)

    @property
    def head(self) -> str:
        """The current chain head (last entry_hash, or the genesis if empty)."""
        return self._entries[-1].entry_hash if self._entries else _GENESIS

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._entries)

    def verify(self) -> None:
        """Recompute the chain; raise :class:`ProvenanceError` on any inconsistency."""
        verify_records(self._entries, secret=self._secret)

    def to_records(self) -> tuple[dict[str, Any], ...]:
        """Serialize to plain dicts (for a persistent store); re-checkable via :func:`verify_records`."""
        return tuple(
            {
                "seq": e.seq,
                "kind": e.kind,
                "summary": e.summary,
                "payload": dict(e.payload),
                "timestamp": e.timestamp,
                "prev_hash": e.prev_hash,
                "entry_hash": e.entry_hash,
            }
            for e in self._entries
        )


def verify_records(
    entries: "list[ProvenanceEntry] | tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]",
    *,
    secret: bytes | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Verify a sequence of entries forms an intact chain (order, linkage, content).

    Pass the same ``secret`` the trail was keyed with; an unkeyed trail verifies with
    ``secret=None``. A keyed trail will not verify under the wrong/absent secret.
    """
    prev = _GENESIS
    for i, e in enumerate(entries):
        if e.seq != i:
            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} has seq {e.seq} (reordered or removed)")
        if e.prev_hash != prev:
            raise ProvenanceError(f"provenance entry {i} prev_hash breaks the chain")
        recomputed = _digest(e.seq, e.kind, e.summary, e.payload, e.timestamp, e.prev_hash, secret)
        if not hmac.compare_digest(recomputed, e.entry_hash):
            raise ProvenanceError(
                f"provenance entry {i} content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)"
            )
        prev = e.entry_hash


def from_records(
    records: "list[Mapping[str, Any]] | tuple[Mapping[str, Any], ...]",
    *,
    secret: bytes | None = None,
) -> tuple[ProvenanceEntry, ...]:
    """Rebuild entries from :meth:`ProvenanceTrail.to_records` output and verify the chain."""
    entries = tuple(
        ProvenanceEntry(
            seq=r["seq"],
            kind=r["kind"],
            summary=r["summary"],
            payload=r["payload"],
            timestamp=r["timestamp"],
            prev_hash=r["prev_hash"],
            entry_hash=r["entry_hash"],
        )
        for r in records
    )
    verify_records(entries, secret=secret)
    return entries

content.text: The fix looks sound: `record()` is now lock-guarded, wrapping the read-compute-append in `self._lock`. Let me find the regression test.

content.text: The test uses 8 threads with a barrier (max contention), 50 records each, then verifies the chain, asserts count, and asserts contiguous seqs. Let me run it and also verify it would catch a regression by testing against an unlocked version.

Now let me verify the test actually catches a regression by simulating the unlocked version (the bug). Without GIL-released sections this could be flaky, so let me confirm the test fails when the lock is removed.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import threading, sys
sys.path.insert(0, 'src')
from assay_engine import provenance as P

# Monkeypatch to simulate the pre-fix unlocked record(): bypass the lock by calling _record_locked directly
trail = P.ProvenanceTrail()
barrier = threading.Barrier(8)
errors = []

def worker(w):
    barrier.wait()
    for i in range(50):
        try:
            # call the unlocked path directly (pre-fix behavior)
            trail._record_locked('k', f'w{w}-{i}', {'w': w, 'i': i})
        except Exception as e:
            errors.append(e)

threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(w,)) for w in range(8)]
for th in threads: th.start()
for th in threads: th.join()

print('len=', len(trail), 'expected=', 400)
seqs = [e.seq for e in trail.entries]
print('contiguous?', seqs == list(range(400)))
try:
    trail.verify()
    print('verify: PASSED (no corruption observed this run)')
except Exception as e:
    print('verify: FAILED ->', type(e).__name__, str(e)[:80])
" 2>&1 | tail -10
input.description: Simulate pre-fix unlocked behavior to confirm corruption

content.content:
len= 400 expected= 400
contiguous? True
verify: PASSED (no corruption observed this run)

content.text: The unlocked path didn't visibly corrupt in this single run. This is because Python's GIL makes the read-compute-append less likely to interleave when there are no I/O/sleep yield points. Let me run the unlocked simulation many times to confirm it CAN fail (which proves the test would catch a regression), since the concern is whether the test is a real guard.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import threading, sys
sys.path.insert(0, 'src')
from assay_engine import provenance as P

def run_unlocked():
    trail = P.ProvenanceTrail()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(8)
    def worker(w):
        barrier.wait()
        for i in range(50):
            trail._record_locked('k', f'w{w}-{i}', {'w': w, 'i': i})
    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(w,)) for w in range(8)]
    for th in threads: th.start()
    for th in threads: th.join()
    seqs = [e.seq for e in trail.entries]
    bad_len = len(trail) != 400
    bad_seq = seqs != list(range(400))
    bad_verify = False
    try:
        trail.verify()
    except Exception:
        bad_verify = True
    return bad_len, bad_seq, bad_verify

fails = {'len':0, 'seq':0, 'verify':0}
N = 200
for _ in range(N):
    bl, bs, bv = run_unlocked()
    if bl: fails['len'] += 1
    if bs: fails['seq'] += 1
    if bv: fails['verify'] += 1
print(f'over {N} unlocked runs:', fails)
print('Python version:', sys.version.split()[0])
" 2>&1 | tail -5
input.description: Run unlocked sim many times to detect corruption rate

content.text: Interesting — even over 200 runs, the unlocked path never corrupts. This is Python 3.14. Let me check whether free-threading (no-GIL) is active, and whether `_digest` releases the GIL. The race window matters: the GIL serializes pure-Python bytecode, so the read-compute-append is effectively atomic per-record under the GIL unless a yield point exists in the compute section.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import sys
print('GIL enabled:', sys._is_gil_enabled() if hasattr(sys,'_is_gil_enabled') else 'n/a (GIL always on)')
print('free-threaded build:', getattr(sys,'flags',None) and getattr(sys.flags,'gil',None))
" 2>&1

content.text: GIL is enabled. So under the GIL, the read-compute-append in `_record_locked` is hard to interleave because `_digest` (hashlib) does release the GIL but the surrounding seq read and append are pure-Python. Let me force the race by inserting a yield point in the compute section to prove the test CATCHES corruption when the operation genuinely interleaves — this confirms the test's validity by demonstrating the race is real and the lock prevents it.

input.description: Force race with yield point, compare locked vs unlocked

content.text:
This is the conclusive proof. With a realistic yield point in the compute window (which the real `_digest`/clock/freeze section can have):

- **UNLOCKED (pre-fix):** `(False, False)` — corrupted: wrong count/non-contiguous seqs AND broken hash chain (verify fails).
- **LOCKED (fix):** `(True, True)` — intact.

This confirms the race is real and the lock is what prevents it. Now, one caveat: the committed regression test uses the default fast clock with no forced yield. Let me confirm whether that test would actually fail without the lock under normal conditions — i.e., whether it's a real guard or only passes because the GIL hides the race. Let me test the committed test body against an unlocked `record()`.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "
import threading, sys
sys.path.insert(0, 'src')
from assay_engine import provenance as P

# Reproduce the EXACT committed test but route record() to the unlocked path (pre-fix).
# Run it 50x to estimate how reliably the committed test catches the regression.
def committed_test_unlocked():
    t = P.ProvenanceTrail()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(8)
    def worker(w):
        barrier.wait()
        for i in range(50):
            t._record_locked('k', f'w{w}-{i}', {'w': w, 'i': i})
    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(w,)) for w in range(8)]
    for th in threads: th.start()
    for th in threads: th.join()
    # the three assertions of the committed test:
    a1 = len(t) == 400
    try:
        t.verify(); a2 = True
    except Exception: a2 = False
    a3 = [e.seq for e in t.entries] == list(range(400))
    return a1 and a2 and a3  # True == test would PASS (i.e. NOT catch the bug)

passes = sum(committed_test_unlocked() for _ in range(50))
print(f'committed-test-against-unlocked: passed {passes}/50 (passes mean the test FAILED to catch the bug)')
" 2>&1 | tail -3
input.description: Estimate if committed test catches regression with default clock

content.content: committed-test-against-unlocked: passed 50/50 (passes mean the test FAILED to catch the bug)

content.text:
This is the critical finding. The committed regression test, run against the unlocked (pre-fix) code, **passes 50/50 times** — meaning it does NOT catch the regression it's supposed to guard. Under CPython's GIL, the read-compute-append window in `_record_locked` has no GIL-yielding bytecode that splits the critical read from the append in a way that interleaves with the default fast clock, so the race never manifests in the test as written.

The fix itself is correct and sound (the lock genuinely prevents the race, as proven when a yield point exists). But per the task's explicit criterion: "a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count" as a regression test. Let me double-check that the test passes WITH the fix too (it does, from the earlier run) to confirm the equality.

The earlier pytest run confirmed the committed test passes WITH the lock. And now I've shown it passes WITHOUT the lock (50/50). So the test passes equally with and without the fix → it is not a valid regression guard.

Let me confirm the default clock has no yield point and verify there's no `time.sleep` or I/O in the real record path that would change this conclusion.

input.command:
cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python -c "

cd /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay && grep -rn "sys.setswitchinterval\|setswitchinterval" tests/ src/ 2>/dev/null | head
input.description: Confirm no switch-interval tuning in test

content.content: tiny sha256 GIL release: practically none for small msgs

content.text:
Confirmed. The committed test does not tune `sys.setswitchinterval` nor force any yield point, and with tiny payloads `hashlib.sha256` does not reliably release the GIL. So the read-compute-append is effectively atomic under the GIL for this workload, and the test passes regardless of whether the lock is present.

Let me run the full provenance test file once to confirm nothing else is broken and the fix doesn't regress existing behavior.

All 15 tests pass. Summary of my findings for issue #114.

**The fix is APPROPRIATE:** `record()` now acquires `self._lock` and delegates to `_record_locked`, wrapping the entire read-compute-append (seq/prev_hash read → clock/digest/freeze → append) in a single critical section. This soundly addresses the root cause — the non-atomic check-then-act. I empirically proved the lock works: with a realistic yield point inserted in the compute window, the unlocked path corrupts (broken chain, non-contiguous seqs, verify fails) while the locked path stays intact `(True, True)`. No hack, no over-claim; the docstring honestly states "Thread-safe."

**The fix is NOT COMPLETE** — specifically, the regression test does not actually guard the regression. The committed test `test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency` (tests/test_provenance.py:170) passes equally with AND without the lock:
- WITH lock: passes (pytest, 15/15).
- WITHOUT lock (routing the same 8x50 barrier workload through the unlocked `_record_locked` path): passes 50/50 runs.

Under CPython's GIL (confirmed `sys._is_gil_enabled() == True` on the venv's Python 3.14.5), the read-compute-append window contains no reliable GIL-yielding bytecode for the tiny test payloads (`hashlib.sha256` doesn't release the GIL for small inputs; `datetime.now` is fast), so the race never manifests with the default clock. The test therefore does not satisfy the task's stated bar: "a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count." A valid guard would need to force the interleaving (e.g., a slow/yielding clock or `sys.setswitchinterval` tuning) — which I demonstrated does reliably distinguish locked from unlocked.

input.reasoning:
Issue #114: ProvenanceTrail.record() non-atomic check-then-act (not thread-safe).

APPROPRIATE = true. The fix (src/assay_engine/provenance.py) adds self._lock = threading.Lock() in __init__ and makes record() acquire it before delegating to a new _record_locked(), wrapping the entire read-compute-append (seq=len(self._entries), prev_hash read -> clock/digest/freeze -> self._entries.append) in one critical section. This directly and soundly fixes the root cause with no hack and no over-claim (docstring accurately says 'Thread-safe'). I verified the lock genuinely prevents corruption: with a realistic GIL-yield point in the compute window, the unlocked (pre-fix) path produces wrong count + non-contiguous seqs + verify() failure (False,False), while the locked path stays intact (True,True).

COMPLETE = false. The named regression test test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency (tests/test_provenance.py:170) does NOT actually catch the regression. It passes WITH the fix (pytest 15/15) and ALSO passes WITHOUT the fix: routing the exact same 8-threads x 50-records barrier workload through the unlocked _record_locked path passed 50/50 runs. Under CPython's GIL (sys._is_gil_enabled()==True, Python 3.14.5 in the venv) the read-compute-append window has no reliable GIL-yielding bytecode for the tiny test payloads (hashlib.sha256 doesn't release the GIL for small inputs; datetime.now is fast and the test does not tune sys.setswitchinterval), so the race never manifests with the default clock. Per the task's explicit criterion ('a test that passes equally with/without the fix does NOT count'), this test is not a valid guard. A correct guard must force interleaving (e.g. a yielding/slow injected clock or setswitchinterval tuning), which I showed reliably distinguishes locked from unlocked.
input.evidence:
src/assay_engine/provenance.py: __init__ adds `self._lock = threading.Lock()`; record() does `with self._lock: return self._record_locked(kind, summary, payload)` wrapping the seq/prev_hash read + clock/digest/freeze + append.
tests/test_provenance.py:170-189 test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency (8 threads x 50, Barrier, asserts len==400, t.verify(), contiguous seqs).
Empirical: full file pytest = 15 passed. Committed test body run against UNLOCKED _record_locked path with default clock = passed 50/50 (i.e. fails to catch the bug). With a yielding clock injected: UNLOCKED -> (False,False) corrupted chain; LOCKED -> (True,True) intact — proving the race is real, the lock fixes it, but the committed test's default-clock workload cannot surface it under the GIL.

content.text: Issue #114 confirmed: appropriate=true, complete=false. The lock-based fix soundly addresses the root cause (proven via forced-yield comparison), but the regression test passes equally with and without the lock (50/50 runs unlocked), so it does not actually guard the regression.
